notes.anchor: How sore do you think your rival for the augurate must be
notes.note: An augural vacancy (probably that left by Hortensius's expected death) was being filled; Cicero was a candidate, and Hirrus, Caelius's own electoral rival, was a rival of Cicero's for this priesthood. The augurs interpreted the will of the gods from bird-signs.

notes.anchor: your freedman Philotimus and the estate of Milo
notes.note: After Milo's conviction for the murder of Clodius and his exile to Massilia in 52 BC, his property was sold off; Cicero's freedman Philotimus had bought it up, and Caelius is managing the awkward business of settling with Milo's creditors and connections.
Caelius asks Cicero to write a literary work dedicated to their friendship; such commissions and dedications were a normal currency of Roman aristocratic amicitia.
Greek didaskalia, 'instruction' or 'teaching'; Caelius (showing off a little) asks for a work with some genuinely instructive content, the sort that stays in circulation.
